preFix.title: Laying carpet tiles
preFix.subtitle: Lay modular carpet tiles from the centre of a room
preFix.excerpt: How to lay carpet tiles: finding the centre of the room, setting out from the middle to avoid narrow border cuts, sticking or loose-laying tiles, cutting border tiles with a utility knife, and achieving a neat finish at doorways.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: Carpet tiles divide the floor into 500 x 500 mm or 600 x 600 mm modules that can be replaced individually when worn or stained. They are loose-laid or lightly adhered and require no specialist tools. The key to a good result is starting from the centre rather than from a wall: starting from a wall leads to a wide cut on one side and a narrow sliver on the other. Starting from the centre produces equal-width border tiles on opposite walls.

Carpet tiles (measure the room area in square metres and add 10% for cuts), a tape measure, chalk line or straightedge and pencil, a utility knife with fresh blades, a steel ruler or straight-edge for cutting, and carpet tile adhesive (if the tiles do not have self-adhesive backs). A threshold bar for the doorway.

The floor must be flat, clean, and dry. Fill any dips or hollows with levelling compound; carpet tiles show subfloor defects more than sheet vinyl. Remove existing gripper rod and nail holes, and ensure the subfloor is free of adhesive lumps. Acclimatise the tiles to the room for 24 hours before laying.
Step 2, find the centre and snap setting-out lines

Find the midpoint of each of the four walls and snap chalk lines across the room connecting opposite midpoints. The intersection is the room centre. Dry-lay a row of tiles from the centre to one wall; if the last tile leaves a gap smaller than half a tile, move the
by half a tile in that direction. This avoids awkward slivers at the walls.
Start at the centre and lay tiles outward along both setting-out lines, checking the

as you go. Tiles with arrow markings on the back indicate pile direction. For a uniform look, align all arrows the same way. For a quarter-turned chequerboard, rotate alternate tiles 90 degrees. Press each tile firmly into position; for self-adhesive tiles, peel the release paper and press flat. For loose-lay tiles, use spots of double-sided tape at intervals to prevent creep.
Step 4, cut and fit border tiles

Measure the gap to the wall and mark the cut line on the tile back. Score firmly along the line with the utility knife against a steel ruler; thick tiles may need two or three firm passes before they snap cleanly. At doorways, fit a threshold bar at the transition to the adjacent floor covering and tuck the carpet tile edge under the bar's retaining lip.

attrs.fix: Lift and re-lay the creeping tiles with double-sided tape at corners or use a perimeter bead of carpet tile adhesive.
attrs.cause: Loose-laid without adhesive in high-traffic areas.
attrs.symptom: Tiles shifting and creeping over time

attrs.fix: When ordering, check all tiles carry the same batch number. Mix tiles from different boxes when laying to even out any batch variation.
attrs.cause: Tiles from different production batches with slight colour variation.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
